speaker: interviewer
text: Date, August 28 1996. Survivor, Isaac Bitton. Interviewer Sarah Akerlund. City and state, Woodstock, Illinois. Country, United States of America. Language, English. My name is Sarah Akerlund. Today's date is August 28 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Isaac Bitton. I'm conducting the interview in Woodstock, Illinois, United States of America. Could you tell me your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Isaac Bitton.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell it for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-S-double A-C B-I-T-T-O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same. Isaac Bitton.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Ike.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: March 31st, 1926.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city and the country in which you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Lisbon, Portugal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ike, could you tell me how a little bit about your family when you were small? A little bit about your mother? What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was see Simy Schcron Bitton.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-I-M-Y S-C-H-C-R-O-N. Bitton, B-I-T-T-O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a saint. She was a very petite lady. She was born in the city of Faro in the southern-most province of Portugal, the Algarve. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she bore 10 children, 5 boys and 5 girls. And I only remember seeing her in the kitchen, really, from early-- the early hours of the morning till the late hours in the evening. That's all what she did. And suddenly I remember her cooking, particularly the type of dish that the-- the Jews of Sepharad used to use, the adafina that used to cook Friday nights all night, and-- and be ready for the Sabbath.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, of course, she did not cook on Saturdays, but we had some help to oversee the cooking. But everything was prepared by-- by-- by our mother. And many times, we used to ask her, why don't you open a restaurant? Because everybody would love the adafina. I think the Polish Jews and the Russian Jews had the same type. They call it [NON-ENGLISH]. But the basic-- What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a-- it's-- it's made out of beans, potatoes, eggs, meat, and variety of other spices. And then it cooks from beginning of Friday, the beginning of the Sabbath, when they put it on the fire, until-- until Saturday, when you come-- when we used to come back from-- noon, from the services in Lisbon, the synagogue. Then of course we had this large meal, and there was the adafina. So suddenly I remember that my mother was-- was-- she was always busy, 24 hours a day. If it was not cooking, there was then mending and tending to-- to the needs of such a large household, basically.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was David Tangi Bitton.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: D-A-V-I-D. Tangi's T-A-N-G-I, short for Tangiers, and Bitton, B-I-T-T-O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your father's occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember my father, as a little, little boy when-- before he left the-- the police-- he served for several years with the police. He was the honor-- with the honor guard of the president of Portugal, [NON-ENGLISH]. And later on, he wanted to raise a family. So he left the police, and worked for the Portuguese printing press in Lisbon, in the offices and later on as a-- as a driver. Because in those days, you know, it was-- not everybody was eagerly driving. But my father drove a truck-- a British truck by the way, made by Thorneycroft. And it had pneumatic wheels. It had hard, hard rubber wheels. It was very difficult to drive, you know, and it would take a very strong man to turn the steering, to-- to say the least. And at-- at the time, there was no electrical starter. And the way to start a truck was by means of-- by means of using manual line, or a-- a crank. And my father used to get very upset when the car would not start when he wanted to. And you could see the Moroccan temper raised. But ultimately it would start, and he used to take us for rides many times from the printing press to the different places that he used to drop the Portuguese printing press to the-- from the government.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was a good man. We always respected him very much. He was not the father that really punished the sons or beat-- beats them up. But-- but just his look, because he was a big man, commanded respect. And we all in return had respect to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: On-- on Fridays, father used to come home a little earlier, used to take us to the synagogue. And the-- sometimes the four boys, sometimes only two or three boys. And we used to walk. And that was, for him, most likely the-- the greatest event of the week, because he was a very proud man to walk into the-- with his sons. We lived about half an hour away from the synagogue [NON-ENGLISH], The Gates of Hope. And once in the synagogue, of course, there was not too many families that had this large number of children. Very, very few, as a matter of fact. But for David Bitton to walk in with four of his children, you know, everybody would turn around and take a look. And-- and of course, we would be very proud there to show our faces, et cetera, at the synagogue with-- with dad, you know. And so-- and our house was always like Grand Central Station. Not only were we a large family in numbers, but because of the loving and the caring that existed in those days, then father would always welcome newcomers that we used to meet at the synagogue. Because, after all, he had five girls to deal with. And he certainly was interested in having other boys come in to plan ultimately for a Jewish wedding. And so we had all kinds of guests, primarily, I would say, Sephardic guests, Moroccan Jews that used to immigrate to Portugal. And other-- as a matter of fact, other guest as well, and other members of the family. So we always had a large, large group. But perhaps one of the largest gatherings that I do remember was when my uncle, by the name of Nissim Ohayon-- he was a rabbi-- became the chief rabbi in Malta in La Valletta. He left Lisbon in 1936, because I remember his son Isaac Ohayon was my best friend. We were the same age, and I lost my best pal. But prior to their departure, then, of course, my mother was his sister. And father decided then to make a big farewell party to the Ohayon family before they left for Malta. And, again, the-- the primary dish was the adafina. And we-- we really had a great deal of [NON-ENGLISH], happiness. We-- they spoke, I don't say perfect, but some Ladino, in many cases, the old Spanish language. And of course there was a lot of Ladino singing, which is primarily not hardly used anymore, nowadays. But in those days, you still could hear the Ladino in the synagogue among some of the members of the community, as well as guests that used to come to Portugal.

speaker: interviewer
text: So our family relationship was-- was outstanding, you know. And along with that, the-- the Jewish community of Lisbon had many members that came from Gibraltar. And the majority of Gibraltar and Morocco-- so Spanish-- the old Spanish Ladino was used quite a bit. And of course-- but once in Portugal, they would speak the native Portuguese-- Portuguese language. So it was a very, very happy family. You mentioned that there were 10 of you. 10 children. Do you remember the-- the names of everybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. My-- my oldest brother, god bless his soul, name was Messod. M-E-S-S-O-D. Myself, the second child. The third child was Joseph, who is alive in Lisbon still. And the fourth one was my brother Abraham, god bless his soul. He was injured in the War of Independence in Israel and died later on, not as a result of the war, but he died in Tel Aviv. And then came my sister, Judith. Then Tano, Hana, Leah, Rachel, and the last child was Jacob. So these are the 10 children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What school did you attend in Lisbon?

speaker: interviewee
text: I attended two schools. And also we attended a private tutor, who taught us Hebrew. Primarily was not speaking Hebrew, but Hebrew that-- so that we could read from the prayer books in the Hebrew language. And, of course, you picked up some words in-- in Hebrew as well, that you could communicate with. But I attended the-- the public school in Lisbon. But, please forgive me, you asked me the name before and I could not remember the name of the-- that public school. But then I attended the Hebrew school, which was taught by-- I don't remember her name. Ironically, she was-- she was an immigrant from Poland who taught Hebrew. Again, this-- at this point in time, it was not just Hebrew to-- to-- to learn or read the prayer books, but it was also spoken Hebrew. So we learned a little about that. It was probably before we left anyway. Of interesting, perhaps, in those days, remembering our childhood, was the summer time. We came from a poor family, obviously. And the Jewish community of Lisbon used to take good, good care of all its children, the rich as well as the poor. So they had a place outside Lisbon, approximately-- maybe 45 minutes to one hour's drive, by the name of Paco, P-A-C-O, de Arco, A-R-C-O-S. Arcos. And this was along the seashore. And-- and they used to have a place for us. And we used to spend approximately 10 days on vacation there. We had some-- two guardians, primarily, and we used to have a hell of-- of a time. We used to give a very hard time to the people that used to watch over us, obviously. And at those-- in those days, there was already new children from immigrants, as well. And we had a very difficult time to communicate because of the different languages that they spoke. But amongst the-- the Portuguese Jewish community, we, of course, had no-- no problems. We used to have pillow fights, and this type of things. And to aggravate our tutor and-- and-- but other than that, we used to have very, very good time. Other things that the community did for us is, once a year, particularly during the high holidays, they would-- they would buy us new clothing, like a uniform. Blue suits, shoes, shirts, ties, everything, to all the poor of the community. And-- and we always waited for that occasion, because other than that, with ten children, we had to use the shoes from one brother to another one, or-- or the pants, or the shirt, and so forth. So once a year, we knew that we're going to be dressed from scratch, so to speak. And make us very happy. And so life in general amongst the Jewish community and Lisbon was-- was nice and easy, so to speak, other than the fact that we had to go to work to help the family at a little early age. And my first job, which my father was able to get from me, was to dismantle sea mines from World War I. I mean, these were, of course, not dangerous, because all you had to do is they had probably a million screws and nuts and bolts, and we had just to take that apart. And everything was rusty, and it was difficult to do. But we spent several days there. And dad would give us money. Dad would give us money, then, to go to the movies. And the rest would stay at home for-- for the family needs. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other favorite religious holidays that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we-- we-- obviously, there was quite a few. But perhaps one of the-- the holidays that we always were looking forward to was Purim, the feast of Esther, Queen Esther. And at-- in-- in those days, we used to call upon the businesses-- primarily Jewish businesses office, and we just came in, and immediately they had prepared already the coins. You know, rolls and rolls of coins for the-- for the gifts for the-- the children that knock at the door.

speaker: interviewer
text: So many times we used to go-- to go in, and-- and turn around, and come back a little later on. And we used to gather two or three times coins from-- from these places. And, then, of course, again the money probably would go home ultimately, but whatever we could spend in goodies that otherwise we didn't have money to, we would. So we had a very, very good time with that. Did you have friends that were not Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many. Majority of the Portuguese, of course, are-- are Christians, are Catholics, very religious. And we had many of those friends. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large was the Jewish population in Lisbon when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Within-- at-- at that point in time, within 800. Later on, during the war years, then it increased to probably 1,200 to 1,500.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that there were immigrants that were coming in to Lisbon in those early years. Were they coming because of anti-Semitism? Or do you know why they were coming in those early years to Lisbon?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were many Jewish immigrants that came to Portugal prior to the Second World War, and primarily Russian and Polish Jews. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I cannot really for certainly say, yes, it was because-- due to anti-Semitism. A little bit later on, shortly before the World War, II, then of course you had many immigrants that came from, again, Germany and Poland, Hungary, Romanian, from almost all over Europe. There was a question some time why did the Prime Minister Salazar of Portugal act so bitterly against a man who had saved so many lives, so many Jewish lives, which was the Portuguese consul Dr. Aristedes de Sousa Mendes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And it's often said that the main reason was that Salazar had to decide between which of the two were the greatest evils, communism or Nazism. And-- and the result was he chose to work with the Nazis, I suppose. Hitler. He became a friend of Hitler. Hitler sent him a brand new car, a Mercedes Benz, as a gift to Salazar. And so to answer your question, if it was due to anti-Semitism, it certainly had to do with the fact that the Germans did not particularly care for the Jews. But for that matter, neither did the Russians, or-- or other countries either.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel anti-Semitism when you were growing up as a boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: To answer that, I would say no. As a Portuguese Jew, the only incident that I could say that had some-- tasted-- tastes, if you wish, of anti-Semitism, was when the-- the Portuguese consul in Bordeaux, France, Dr. Aristedes de Sousa Mendes, was expulsed by the government of Portugal simply because he had helped thousands of Jews to pass through Spain with a Portuguese visa into neutral Portugal, and from there into freedom. So that would be perhaps classified as an act of anti-Semitism, but not by the Portuguese people. It is my opinion, and of others as well, that the-- the main reason was because there were some people in the Portuguese hierarchy of the government, perhaps including the leader, Salazar, as well, that they didn't care for the Jews, because they were friends with the Germans, with the Nazis. And as a result, they simply make life very difficult for the Jews. But the Portuguese people themselves, the people alone, they-- they-- they wouldn't even know what-- what anti-Semitism means-- means. You know, there's a large portions of the Portuguese people that are related to the Jews simply because of the-- the Inquisition times, you know, when they-- when the expulsion in 1492 occurred in Spain, it's often said that over 200,000, 300,000 Jews were expelled or run away or-- or took refuge in Portugal. So when you have such a large number amongst-- the population in Portugal at the time was in the-- within a million, then obviously through mixed marriages, there was a large percentage of the population of Portugal-- even today, it is said often that perhaps 30% of the Portuguese have some blood ties to the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your family-- or did your family do anything to help the immigrants that were coming in? Do you remember them, as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, most of the small Jewish community helped, and in a variety of ways. In-- in our case, in our family, my aunt Esther, God bless her soul, was the manager of the Jewish soup kitchen, the [NON-ENGLISH]. And obviously, by-- by managing the kitchen-- and there was a-- a-- a hall specifically for the refugees, and there was another small hall for the poor of the community. I remembered one time that one lady, which I do not remember the name, was a German Jewish refugee. She was the mother of two, a boy and a girl. A small girl. Maybe she was 18 to-- months to two years old. The mother had a-- suffered a mental breakdown, perhaps through the stress of the war, perhaps because she was a refugee of the Holocaust. And she was hospitalized at the [NON-ENGLISH], next to the soup kitchen. There was a Jewish hospital. And her daughter-- my-- my aunt Esther took care of her little child and helped-- helped her while the mother was in the-- in the hospital recuperating. Later on, by the way, that was the main cause that when I immigrated to Israel, that they asked me-- they invited me to come with them to Nahariya and stay with their family, which I remember the name was Graumann, G-R-A-U-M-A-N-N, also German Jews. So in as much as my input as a teenager helping refugees, it was primarily in leading them to some of the residences, leading them to some of the shops. In the area of Lisbon they were helping refugees, I remember a family by the name of [NON-ENGLISH]. Mother, father, two children, also German Jews. And he decided to set up a little shop in the center of Lisbon on the street, by means of a table approximately three feet by three feet. And he used to sell-- sell razor blades and small utensils. And because of his problem with the communication with the Portuguese, and the language, then I would help him. And-- and I understood already some-- I was learning some German words and Portuguese words. So I used to tell him how to pronounce it in Portuguese. So basically our input in helping to that nature. There was a gentleman by the name of [NON-ENGLISH], also a German Jew, who was the coordinator between the refugees and the-- the Jewish soup kitchen, and the hospital. He was always in that area.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they would ask us, well, why don't you, in the broken Portuguese, because we really could not speak German-- I couldn't. I do now, but not then-- he used to ask us to help. Why don't you go with this fellow? Why don't you go with this lady and show them where the-- the street is, and the apartment is, or whatever they needed. And that's how we were helping them. And at the soup kitchen also I was helping in setting up the tables and the chairs and so forth. Had the borders to-- from Spain been closed so these people had a difficult time getting into Portugal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very definitely. Through the friendship between Hitler and Generalissimo Franco of Spain, they closed the borders between Spain and France in several areas, particularly in the south of France or the north part of Spain, in [NON-ENGLISH]. [BACKGROUND NOISE] So-- and that made it difficult to cross through-- through Spain into Portugal.
